Art title 1: Station 6 Jesus is Scourged and Crowned with Thorns
Artist Statement 1: The tearing whip that scourged Jesus and the crown of thorns thatgouged him surround Jesus and his attackers. Teeth have beenknocked out and a snake whispers anger and hate in his ear. Like alamb at the mercy of wolves, he does not fight back. The feeling ofbeing hated and beaten unfairly when no one is there to stop itreminded me of a certain boy who was tied to a fencepost andbeaten for being different in a remote area of the United States afew years back.
Story Title again: ANIMATION: Station 6 Jesus is Scourged and Crowned with Thorns
excerpt:
(See video below) Then Pilate took Jesus and had him scourged. And the soldiers wove a crown out of thorns and placed it on his head, and clothed him in a purple cloak, and they came to him and said, "Hail, King of the Jews!" And they struck him repeatedly.
 (John 19: 1--3, NAB)


Lord, grant us patience in times of suffering, that we may offer our lives as a sacrifice of praise.


First let's look at some of the physical pain Jesus felt. Look at the sharp thorns that were pressed into his head. Look at where his back was whipped. Severe, acute pain, over and over. So many times when he hoped it was over, it came again. When have you or someone very close to you had to bear intense or perhaps prolonged physical pain? From a disease, an injury, or an accident?

Or perhaps it was verbal abuse? Jesus felt something similar on this day.

Now look at the cat-o'-nine-tails, perhaps like the one used. It was maybe built of rope and metal, things that if used for good can be great tools. But here these have been modified to tear the human body. Do some people lash out at you with a sharp tongue? When and with whom does this happen for you? If you choose to color this, think of things that were said as you color each coil of the rope or each claw of metal. Jesus felt this too.

Now let's imagine how the social dynamics might have felt. Jesus, alone, with no one to defend him, was entirely at the mercy of those who hated him. They could do what they wanted, out of sheer hate. It reminds me of what Matthew Shepard might have felt or African Americans surrounded by powerful whites in times of intense persecution. Maybe these soldiers took out on him their fear or dissatisfaction in their own lives. Oh, how good it could feel to throw our pain onto someone else who is condemned anyway. Perhaps we appear more civilized now, but do you act on similar impulses in small ways to those who can't or won't fight back?

And lastly look at this same dynamic shown through other symbols: a sheep totally helpless before wolves. Do we take out our frustrations on those we have power over? Friends who need us? Our children? Elderly family members? Coworkers? Do we take little jabs at them with our tone, knowing they can't or won't fight back? Where do you see this in your life?

Lord, forgive us.


And does this help you love Jesus more? Think of how he was abused for you. He bore it because he knew it could have the power to draw you away from your selfishness and into closer union with him.

Think back to the day John the Baptist saw Jesus coming toward him and recognized him for who he was. John told his disciples, "Behold, the lamb of God, who takes away the sin of the world."

This lamb went to the slaughter, bearing up all the violence of these soldiers, and the vitriol and cheap shots and angry little quips that come from us to others. He died as an offering before God--a sacrifice to pay for the little and big moments of impatience and anger we inflict on those around us. He says, "I forgive you. But now, with a clean slate follow me. I ask you to carry your cross of patience and kindness and humility, and I will help make you new."

Amen.
